content.assessment: This proposal isolates a distinct causal channel (zoning/choice/legal exemptions) from track-admissions reform, which in principle allows more modular, containable implementation steps (e.g., discrete legal review of maintainer exemptions). But the lens flags that municipal planning autonomy — the actor capacity needed for zoning-level intervention — has itself been curtailed by centralization, creating a capacity mismatch between the proposed local levers and the current governance structure.
opportunities: Legal review of maintainer exemptions could proceed as a contained pilot-scale action independent of broader systemic capacity constraints
Could be sequenced ahead of admissions or de-tracking reforms as a lower-risk first step, testing institutional appetite for structural change
strengths: Legal lever (review of anti-discrimination exemptions) is a discrete, boundable implementation unit distinct from broader admissions reform, aiding sequencing clarity
Precedent exists for administrative desegregation rules producing at least temporary measurable effects, demonstrating some proof-of-concept deliverability
threats: Legal challenge risk given prior Curia rulings favoring maintainer exemption, creating a specific containment failure mode if litigation reverses gains
Governance mismatch between where zoning authority formally sits (local) and where planning capacity has been concentrated (central) threatens delivery even if the legal lever is nominally available
weaknesses: Centralization under KLIK has curtailed municipal planning autonomy, directly undermining the local-actor capacity this proposal's zoning mechanism depends on (F-live-implementation_planning-03)
Prior administrative desegregation measures stagnated mid-decade, indicating no durable adaptation loop was previously established for this type of lever
